title: What to know before using Viburnum lantana (Mealytree)?
intro_desc: Viburnum lantana, also known as wayfaring tree, is a versatile shrub valued for its ornamental qualities and medicinal properties. It thrives in well-drained soil and full sun to partial shade, making it a popular choice for gardens and landscapes. The plant produces clusters of white flowers in the spring, followed by clusters of blue-black berries that attract birds and other wildlife. As a medicinal plant, Viburnum lantana has been used in traditional medicine to treat a variety of ailments, including fever, inflammation, and diarrhea. Its bark and berries contain several bioactive compounds, such as tannins and saponins, that are believed to have therapeutic effects. However, it is important to note that all parts of the plant can be toxic if ingested in large quantities, and it should be used with caution.
medicine_intro: Viburnum lantana is used to treat a variety of health conditions, including fever, diarrhea, and bronchial symptoms. Its medicinal uses have been attributed to the presence of active constituents such as tannins, saponins, and flavonoids. The bark and fruit of Viburnum lantana have been used to prepare decoctions, infusions, and tinctures, which have been found to have anti-inflammatory, antispasmodic, and astringent properties. Additionally, this plant has been shown to have analgesic effects and may be useful in treating menstrual cramps and other types of pain. Despite its medicinal benefits, Viburnum lantana can cause side effects such as stomach upset, nausea, and vomiting. It may also interact with certain medications, such as blood thinners and sedatives, and should be used with caution. Pregnant women and individuals with gastrointestinal or kidney problems should avoid using Viburnum lantana, as it may exacerbate these conditions.
medicine_desc: Viburnum lantana has several medicinal uses, such as treating gynecological ailments, reducing fevers, and alleviating symptoms of colds and flu. Its medicinal properties have been valued for centuries, and it is often used as a natural remedy for a variety of health conditions.

The health benefits of Viburnum lantana can be attributed to its medicinal constituents. These include tannins, saponins, flavonoids, and essential oils, which all contribute to the plant's therapeutic properties. Tannins have astringent qualities, making them useful for treating diarrhea and skin irritations. Saponins have anti-inflammatory and expectorant effects, and can help to alleviate respiratory symptoms. Flavonoids have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects, and can help to reduce fever and inflammation. The essential oils in Viburnum lantana have antimicrobial properties and can help to boost the immune system.

Medicinal preparations of Viburnum lantana include teas, tinctures, and decoctions. To make a tea, steep 1-2 teaspoons of dried Viburnum lantana leaves in boiling water for 10-15 minutes. This tea can be consumed up to three times a day to treat diarrhea, sore throat, or fever. A tincture can be made by steeping 1 ounce of dried Viburnum lantana leaves in 1 pint of brandy for two weeks. This tincture can be taken in doses of 1-2 teaspoons up to three times a day to treat respiratory symptoms or menstrual cramps. A decoction can be made by boiling 1 ounce of dried Viburnum lantana bark in 1 quart of water for 30 minutes. This decoction can be consumed throughout the day to treat diarrhea or menstrual cramps.

Despite its many medicinal uses, Viburnum lantana should be used with caution. It can cause stomach upset and nausea in some people, and should not be taken in large doses. It may also interact with certain medications, such as blood thinners and sedatives. Pregnant women should avoid using Viburnum lantana, as it may stimulate uterine contractions.

Precautions should be taken when using Viburnum lantana. It is important to use only dried leaves, bark, or berries, as the fresh plant contains irritating compounds that can cause skin irritation and stomach upset. It is also important to avoid using Viburnum lantana for extended periods of time, as it can cause liver damage. As with any natural remedy, it is always best to consult with a healthcare provider before using Viburnum lantana. They can provide guidance on dosage, precautions, and potential interactions with other medications.
horticulture_intro: Viburnum lantana needs full sun to partial shade and well-drained soil to flourish. When planting, ensure the root ball is slightly above the surrounding soil to promote proper drainage. This plant prefers regular watering, but it's essential to let the soil dry out between waterings to prevent root rot. To keep Viburnum lantana healthy, prune it after flowering to remove spent blooms and shape the plant. When it comes to harvesting, the fruit can be picked when it's fully ripe, which is when it turns deep blue or black. However, be aware that this plant is susceptible to pests such as aphids, scale insects, and borers, and diseases like powdery mildew and leaf spot. Regularly inspect the plant and treat any issues promptly to keep it thriving.
horticulture_desc: Viburnum lantana has specific growth requirements. It thrives in well-drained soil, so it's essential to ensure that the area where you plant it has good drainage. This shrub prefers full sun, but it can tolerate partial shade. It requires a moderate amount of water, and it's essential to water it consistently, so the soil remains moist, but not waterlogged. Viburnum lantana can tolerate various soil types, including clay, loam, and sand, but it prefers slightly acidic to neutral soil pH.

When planting Viburnum lantana, it's important to choose a location with enough space for the shrub to grow, as it can reach up to 12 feet in height and width. It's best to plant it in the spring or fall, and it should be spaced at least 6-8 feet apart. Before planting, make sure to remove any weeds or grass from the area and dig a hole that's twice as wide and as deep as the root ball. Place the shrub in the hole, making sure that the top of the root ball is level with the surrounding soil, and backfill the hole with soil. Water it thoroughly after planting.

Caring for Viburnum lantana is relatively easy. It benefits from regular watering and fertilization. It's best to water it once a week, providing enough water to soak the soil around the shrub's roots. However, it's important to avoid overwatering, as this can cause root rot. Fertilization should be done in the spring, using a slow-release fertilizer. It's important to prune Viburnum lantana regularly to maintain its shape and promote new growth. Pruning should be done in the late winter or early spring, removing any dead or damaged branches.

Harvesting Viburnum lantana can be done year-round, but it's best to wait until the berries are fully ripe, which is in the late summer or early fall. The berries can be used for various purposes, such as making jellies or jams, or for medicinal purposes. When harvesting, it's important to wear gloves, as the berries can stain your hands. It's also essential to remember that only the berries are edible; the leaves and stems are toxic if ingested.

Pests and diseases can affect Viburnum lantana, so it's important to monitor the shrub regularly. Common pests include aphids, scale, and spider mites. These pests can be controlled with horticultural oil or insecticidal soap. Viburnum lantana can also be affected by powdery mildew, which can be controlled with fungicides. Regularly checking the shrub for any signs of pests or diseases can help prevent any significant damage. If any issues are noticed, it's important to address them promptly to keep the shrub healthy and thriving. Overall, Viburnum lantana is a hardy and versatile shrub that can add beauty and interest to any landscape with proper care and attention.
botany_intro: Viburnum lantana belongs to the Adoxaceae family and is a deciduous shrub that can grow up to 6 meters tall. Its morphology includes opposite, simple leaves that are ovate-oblong with serrated margins and a length of 5-10 cm. There are several variants of Viburnum lantana, including the cultivars "Marengo" and "Variegatum," which have distinct leaf patterns and colors. This plant is native to Europe, western Asia, and parts of Africa, typically found in woodlands, hedges, and scrublands. The life-cycle of Viburnum lantana involves producing clusters of white flowers in the spring, followed by small, blue-black berries in the fall, which are a valuable food source for birds.
botany_desc: Viburnum lantana is a species of flowering plant that belongs to the family Viburnaceae. This plant is a part of the genus Viburnum, which contains over 150 species of shrubs and small trees. Viburnum lantana is native to Europe and western Asia, and it is a member of the Adoxaceae family, which includes plants such as Sambucus (elder) and Hamamelis (witch hazel). The plant's scientific name, Viburnum, comes from the Latin word "viere" which means "to bind," referring to the plant's use in binding and wattle work.

Morphologically, Viburnum lantana is a deciduous shrub that can grow up to 6 meters tall. It has a multi-stemmed habit, with ascending branches that form a broad, round-topped head. The plant's leaves are opposite, simple, and ovate, with serrated margins and a pointed tip. The leaves are dark green and glossy on the upper surface, and they are covered with fine hairs on the lower surface. The plant's flowers are white, and they are arranged in flat-topped clusters called cymes. Each flower has five petals and a cluster of stamens in the center. The fruit of the plant is a small, round drupe that turns dark blue or black when it is ripe.

Viburnum lantana is also known by several variant names, including wayfaring tree, European cranberrybush, and Guelder rose. These names reflect the plant's diverse range of attributes and uses. For example, the name "wayfaring tree" comes from the plant's use as a landmark for travelers, while "European cranberrybush" refers to the plant's edible fruit. The name "Guelder rose" comes from the Dutch province of Gelderland, where the plant is native. Despite these variant names, there are no known differences between the plants that they refer to; they are all the same species of Viburnum lantana.

Viburnum lantana is native to Europe and western Asia, and it is found in a wide range of habitats, including forests, hedges, and rocky slopes. The plant is particularly common in the understory of deciduous forests, where it receives partial shade. It is also found in disturbed areas, such as roadsides and railway cuttings. The plant is tolerant of a wide range of soil types, including both acidic and alkaline soils, and it can grow in both wet and dry conditions.

The life cycle of Viburnum lantana begins in the spring, when the plant produces new shoots from its base. These shoots grow quickly, and they produce leaves and flowers by the summer. The plant's flowers are pollinated by insects, and they produce fruit in the fall. The fruit is eaten by birds, which disperse the seeds in their droppings. The seeds can also be spread by water, and they can remain viable in the soil for several years. The plant is hardy, and it can survive in temperatures as low as -20°C. It is also resistant to diseases and pests, making it a popular choice for landscaping and gardening.
history_intro: Viburnum lantana has been used historically for medicinal purposes. The fruit, bark, and leaves of this shrub have been used in herbal medicine to treat a variety of ailments, such as fever, diarrhea, and skin conditions. According to Greek mythology, the nymphs of Mount Parnassus transformed themselves into mealytree branches to escape the pursuits of the god Apollo. The branches of this plant were often used in ancient rituals as a symbol of modesty and chastity. In literature, Viburnum lantana has been mentioned in the works of Pliny the Elder and other early naturalists. The plant is also known for its symbolism, representing quietness, retirement, and thoughtfulness.
history_desc: Viburnum lantana has several historical medicinal uses, such as a treatment for fever and various skin issues. The plant's bark and roots have been used in traditional medicine to make teas, decoctions, and poultices to alleviate symptoms of ailments such as ague, diarrhea, and skin diseases. Its bark has also been used to make a tonic that was believed to promote menstruation and childbirth. The plant's medicinal properties are attributed to its various chemical components, including tannins, saponins, and alkaloids, which have been found to have anti-inflammatory, analgesic, and astringent effects.

In Greek mythology, Viburnum lantana is associated with the story of Hippomenes and Atalanta. According to the myth, Atalanta, a huntress, was exceptionally swift and challenged potential suitors to a race, with the condition that she would marry the winner. However, she was undefeated until she encountered Hippomenes, who sought the help of the goddess Aphrodite. The goddess gave him three golden apples, which he used to distract Atalanta during the race, causing her to slow down and allowing Hippomenes to win. After their victory, the two made love in a sacred Viburnum lantana grove, and it is said that their union produced Parthenope, the mythical founder of the city of Naples.

Viburnum lantana has also been used in ancient rituals as a protective and purifying plant. The plant's branches have been used to make wreaths and garlands to adorn doorways and shrines, and the leaves and berries have been burnt as incense to purify sacred spaces. The plant's protective properties have also been attributed to its ability to repel insects and other pests. In some cultures, the plant is also believed to have mystical powers and is used in divination and spell-casting.

In literature, Viburnum lantana has been mentioned in various works, including poetry and prose. The plant's symbolism and mythological associations have made it a popular subject in Romantic literature, with poets such as John Keats and Percy Bysshe Shelley referencing it in their works. In Shelley's "Adonais," for instance, the plant is used metaphorically to symbolize the beauty and transience of life. The plant has also been featured in works of botanical literature, such as John Gerard's "The Herball or General Historie of Plantes," where it is described as a "handsome shrub" with "pretty red berries."

Finally, Viburnum lantana has been used as a symbol in various contexts. Its red berries, for example, have been used as a symbol of fertility and abundance, while its branches have been used as a symbol of protection and purification. The plant's association with the mythological story of Hippomenes and Atalanta has also given it a romantic connotation, and it is sometimes used as a symbol of love and marriage. In some cultures, the plant is also believed to have spiritual significance, with its branches used in rituals to communicate with the dead and to invoke the protection of the gods. Overall, Viburnum lantana's historical and cultural significance has made it a plant of great interest and value, both in medicinal and symbolic contexts.
